FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 26, 2006

Kate J. Hart
701 University Avenue, Suite 200
Sacramento, CA 95825

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-06-030

Dear Ms. Hart:

Enclosed please find a corrected version of our advice letter issued in File No.
I-06-030. This amended letter is issued to correct footnote no. 4 appearing on page 4 and
to add footnote 6 that should have been included on page five. Please disregard the
previous letter and replace with this corrected advice letter.

If you have any questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

VJ:rd
Enclosure
I:\AdviceLtrs\I-06-030
Corrected Letter 4/26/05

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 18, 2006

Kate J. Hart
701 University Avenue, Suite 200
Sacramento, CA 95825

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-06-030

Dear Ms. Hart:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ As your request does not
identify a specific governmental decision, we are treating it as a request for informal
assistance.² This letter is solely based on the facts presented to us in your request. The
Commission does not act as a finder of fact when issuing advice or informal assistance.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Our advice is applicable only to the extent that
the facts provided to us are correct, and all of the material facts have been provided.

QUESTION

Do the conflicts-of-interest provisions of the Act preclude you from participating
in decisions of the California Regional Water Quality Control Board, Central Valley
Region, on matters involving clients of your future spouse's lobbying firm?

CONCLUSION

As a public official, you will be prohibited from making, participating in making,
or using, or attempting to use your position as a member of the California Regional Water
Quality Control Board, Central Valley Region, to influence any decision involving any
client of your future spouse's firm if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on either the firm or the firm's clients.

___________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c), copy enclosed.)

File No. I-06-030
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are a member of the California Regional Water Quality Control Board,
Central Valley Region ("Regional Board"). You are engaged to be married to an
individual who owns 100% of a lobbying firm (the "Firm"). The Firm specializes in
water quality and environmental issues and frequently represents clients before the State
Water Resources Control Board and various regional water quality control boards.
Although the Firm does not currently represent clients before the Regional Board, it has
represented clients before the Regional Board in the past and it is certainly possible (even
foreseeable) that it may do so in the future.

You and your fiancé intend to enter into a marital property agreement (the
"Marital Agreement") on the date of your marriage. The Marital Agreement will provide
that each of your and your fiancé's current separate property will remain so, and that
future income you each may derive will also retain separate property status.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act provides that "[n]o public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his
official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to
know he has a financial interest." Regulations 18700 through 18709 set forth an eight-
step analysis in determining whether a public official has a conflict of interest in a
governmental decision he or she will be making.

Step One. Are you a "public official" within the meaning of section 87100?

Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." You have correctly identified yourself
as a public official as a member of the California Regional Water Quality Control Board,
Central Valley Region, which is a state governmental agency.

Step Two. Are you making, participating in making or influencing a
governmental decision?

In your letter, you state that as a member of the Regional Board, you will
make, participate in making or use your official position to influence decisions of the
Regional Board.³

³ If, after completing the eight-step analysis to a governmental decision, you conclude that you
have a conflict of interest with respect to that particular decision, we would direct your attention to
regulation 18702.5(b). It provides that if a public official who holds an office specified in section 87200
has a conflict-of-interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: (1) immediately prior to
the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well
as details of the economic interest on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave
the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars,
absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in
sections 18702.5(c) and 18702.5(d) apply.

File No. I-06-030
Page No. 3

Step Three. What are the economic interests? What are the possible sources
of financial conflict-of-interest?

The economic interests from which conflicts of interest may arise are described in
section 87103 and regulations 18703-18703.5. There are six kinds of economic interests:

1. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he
or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a);
regulation 18703.1(a).)

2. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b).)

3. A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); regulation
18703.2.)

4. An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87103(c); regulation 18703.3.)

5. A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts total $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e);
regulation 18703.4.)

6. A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is
commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; regulation
18703.5.)

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of section 87100:

"For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any
investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public
official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity
or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent
children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or
greater."

In addition, a public official's "income" for conflict-of-income purposes includes
his or her community property interest in the income of his or her spouse. (Section
82030(a).)

File No. I-06-030
Page No. 4

Under the facts you have provided, you have at least four relevant economic
interests.

Investment Interest

First, once your fiancé becomes your spouse, you are deemed to have an
economic (indirect investment) interest in the Firm if your fiancé has a 10% or greater
interest in the Firm and he has invested $2,000 or more in the Firm, which presumably,
he has. (Section 87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a).) Since the definition of "investment"
includes an ownership interest owned by a public official or his or her immediate family
(i.e. your spouse and dependent children), you could not eliminate your economic
(investment) interest in the firm owned by your fiancé through the Marital Agreement.
(Section 82034; Dillon Advice Letter, No. I-02-082; Perrott Advice Letter, No. I-00-
141.)

Sources of Income

Second, under section 87103(c) and regulation 18703.3 you will have an interest
in the Firm as a source of income if you receive a community property share of income
from the Firm totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to participating in a
governmental decision. This is because "income" includes any community property
share of income received by the spouse of an official. (Section 82030.)

Third, your interest in the Firm also includes a pro-rata share of income of the
spouse's business from clients where a spouse owns 10% or more of a business entity.
Thus, since your future spouse owns 100 percent of the Firm, any client who has paid the
Firm $1,000 or more (making your community property share of your future spouse's
interest in the income at least $500) is a "source of income" as set forth in section
87103(c).

These two economic interests, however, will not be a basis for a conflict of
interest, assuming that your Marital Agreement successfully divests you of your
community property interest in the Firm's income.⁴ (Morales Advice Letter, No. A-99-
246(a); Shaw Advice Letter, No. I-90-377.) For this reason, we will end our analysis of
the Firm's clients as a source of income to you at this step.

Personal Financial Effects

Fourth, with respect to personal financial effects on your household, regulation
18704.5 provides that "[a] pubic official or his or her immediate family is deemed to be
directly involved in a governmental decision which has any financial effect on his or her
personal finances or those of his or her immediate family." Therefore, if a governmental

⁴ Our advice is based on the assumption that the Marital Agreement will be a valid and binding
agreement under the relevant laws governing such agreements. (See Perrott, supra; Morales, supra; Vassey
Advice Letter, No. A-86-201.)

File No. I-06-030
Page No. 5

decision will have a personal financial effect upon you or your future spouse, you will be
deemed to be directly involved in that decision. (Section 87103; regulation 18703.5.)

Step Four. Will your economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in
a governmental decision?

Regulation 18704.1 provides that a person, including business entities …"is
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either
directly or by an agent: (1) [i]nitiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made
by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; (2) [i]s a named party in, or
is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the official or the
official's agency. A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."

The facts you have provided assume that the Firm has not and will not be
initiating any proceeding in which you will be making a decision by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request, nor will the Firm be a named party, or be the subject of,
any proceeding concerning a decision before you or the Regional Board. That being the
case, decisions of the Regional Board would only indirectly affect your investment
interest in the Firm.5

Also, as stated above, if a decision by the Regional Board will have a personal
financial effect upon your spouse, you are deemed to be directly involved in that
decision. (Section 87103; regulation 18703.5.)

Step Five. Materiality. What kinds of financial impacts on your economic
interest are considered important enough to trigger a conflict of interest?

The materiality standard applicable to economic interests in business entities
indirectly involved in a governmental decision is set forth in regulation 18705.1(c). For
purposes of this analysis we will assume that the Firm is not a listed Fortune 500
company, is not listed on any of the national stock exchanges and is not qualified for
such listings by virtue of its income.6 Using that assumption, regulation 18705.1(c)(4)
provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on the business is material if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision: (a) "…will result in an
increase or decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more; or, (b) …will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal
year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or, (c)… will result in an increase or decrease in

__________
5 If that is not the case, we direct your attention to regulation 18705.1(a)(1) and 18705.1(b).

6 If the business is not listed on the NASDAQ or the American Stock Exchange but for its most
recent fiscal year had net income of at least $500,000, or earnings before taxes of at least $750,000,
materiality is determined by regulation 18705.1(c)(3).

File No. I-06-030
Page No. 6

the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more." This is the
standard you would apply to decisions of the Regional Board that affect the Firm.

Regulation 18705.5(a) sets forth the materiality standard for economic interests in
personal finances:

"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's
personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month
period. When determining whether a governmental decision has
a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest
in his or her personal finances, neither a financial effect on the
value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the official,
nor a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value of
assets and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has
a direct or indirect investment interest shall be considered."

Because a financial effect on the value of the gross revenues, expenses, or value
of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which you have a direct or indirect interest
is not considered for purposes of your economic interest in your personal finances, any
financial effect on the Firm (a business entity), will be disregarded.

Step Six. Is it substantially likely that the governmental decisions in which
you will participate will result in the applicable materiality standards being met? If
yes, a conflict of interest exists.

An effect upon economic interest is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops 198.)

Using the same assumptions we used in Step Five, we conclude that you would be
disqualified from participating in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will result in: a) an increase or decrease in the Firm's gross revenues for a
fiscal year in an amount of $20,000 or more; or (b) the Firm incurring or avoiding
additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $5,000 or more; or, (c) an increase or decrease in the value of the Firm's
assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more. You must analyze each potential decision on
these terms.

Steps Seven & Eight. If you have a conflict, does the "public generally"
exception apply or may you participate because your participation is legally
required?

Section 87103 (regulation 18707) and section 87101 (regulation 18708) provide
exceptions under which a public official who will experience a material financial effect
as a result of a governmental decision, may nevertheless participate. The facts you have

File No. 1-06-030
Page No. 7

provided suggest that the effect of a Regional Board decision upon a client of the Firm
will not be substantially similar to its effect on the public generally. There is also no
suggestion in the facts you have provided that your participation in any Regional Board
decision involving a client of the Firm will be legally required. For these reasons, we
consider steps seven and eight to be not applicable.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure
VJ:rd

Corrected Letter 4/26/06

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do the conflicts-of-interest provisions of the Act preclude you from participating
in decisions of the California Regional Water Quality Control Board, Central Valliy
Region, on matters involving clients of your future spouse's lobbying firm?

CONCLUSION: As a public official, you will be prohibited from making, participating in making,
or using, or attempting to use your position as a member of the California Regional Water
Quality Control Board, Central Valley Region, to influence any decision involving any
client of your future spouse's firm if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on either the firm or the firm's clients.
I Government Code sections 8 I 000 - g1}l4. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c), copy enclosed.)
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 April 26, 2006 Kate J. Hart 701 University Avenue, Suite 200 Sacramento, CA 95825 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-06-030 Dear Ms. Hart: Enclosed please find a corrected version of our advice letter issued in File No. I-06-030. This amended letter is issued to correct footnote no. 4 appearing on page 4 and to add footnote 6 that should have been included on page five. Please disregard the previous letter and replace with this corrected advice letter. If you have any questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. Sincerely, Luisa Menchaca General Counsel [signature] By: Valentina Joyce Counsel, Legal Division VJ:rd Enclosure I:\AdviceLtrs\I-06-030 Corrected Letter 4/26/05 FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 April 18, 2006 Kate J. Hart 701 University Avenue, Suite 200 Sacramento, CA 95825 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-06-030 Dear Ms. Hart: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of- interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ As your request does not identify a specific governmental decision, we are treating it as a request for informal assistance.² This letter is solely based on the facts presented to us in your request. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when issuing advice or informal assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Our advice is applicable only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and all of the material facts have been provided. QUESTION Do the conflicts-of-interest provisions of the Act preclude you from participating in decisions of the California Regional Water Quality Control Board, Central Valley Region, on matters involving clients of your future spouse's lobbying firm? CONCLUSION As a public official, you will be prohibited from making, participating in making, or using, or attempting to use your position as a member of the California Regional Water Quality Control Board, Central Valley Region, to influence any decision involving any client of your future spouse's firm if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on either the firm or the firm's clients. ___________________ ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c), copy enclosed.) File No. I-06-030 Page No. 2 FACTS You are a member of the California Regional Water Quality Control Board, Central Valley Region ("Regional Board"). You are engaged to be married to an individual who owns 100% of a lobbying firm (the "Firm"). The Firm specializes in water quality and environmental issues and frequently represents clients before the State Water Resources Control Board and various regional
